paper_title: The Convergent Core: Connecting Seed Stability, Cross-Model Universality, and Causal Importance of Sparse Autoencoder Features
email_title: The Convergent Core: Connecting Seed Stability, Cross-Model Universality, and Causal Importance of Sparse Autoencoder Features

sections.summary: This paper studies three axes of sparse autoencoder (SAE) feature quality—seed stability, cross-model universality, and causal importance—on the same feature sets across three language models (GPT-2 Small, Pythia-160M, Pythia-410M). The authors introduce a convergence score that quantifies cross-seed decoder similarity and show it strongly correlates with cross-model feature alignment, while also finding a dissociation: seed-stable (core) features are not individually more causally important than seed-variable (peripheral) features when restricting to active features. They further argue the variable periphery spans a consistent subspace across seeds, suggesting it is structured rather than noise.
sections.strengths:
Technical novelty and innovation
Connects three important dimensions of SAE feature quality—seed stability, universality, and causal importance—in a unified study, which to my knowledge has not been done previously in the LLM setting.
Introduces an explicit convergence score across seeds to quantify feature stability and demonstrates its unique value for predicting cross-model universality without referencing other models at selection time.
Provides a subspace-level analysis showing the peripheral (seed-variable) features collectively form a consistent geometric subspace across seeds, refining the narrative that “instability” is pure noise.


Experimental rigor and validation
Evaluates across three backbone models and five seeds each, with middle-layer activations, and uses both decoder similarity and activation correlations.
Employs nonparametric statistics (Spearman ρ, bootstrap CIs, Mann–Whitney U) and useful ablations (number of seeds, matching strategy, threshold sweeps), lending credibility to the main empirical relationships.
Separates “all features” versus “active-only” analyses, appropriately highlighting confounds from a very high dead-feature fraction.


Clarity of presentation
The motivation and paradox (seed instability vs. cross-model universality) are crisply stated and drive the study design.
Figures are intuitive and support the central claims; the qualitative examples help ground the “core vs. periphery” distinction.


Significance of contributions
If robust, the strong link between seed stability and universality is an actionable insight for comparative interpretability and cross-model alignment studies.
The dissociation between universality and per-feature causal importance encourages more nuanced evaluation of “feature quality” and challenges a common implicit assumption.
sections.weaknesses:
Technical limitations or concerns
High dead-feature fractions (75–91%) due to limited SAE training budget confound many results; although the authors attempt active-only analyses, the overall split into core vs. peripheral is still heavily entangled with liveness.
The causal importance analysis averages KL divergences over all tokens; without conditioning on the feature being active at the token level, this can bias comparisons toward features that simply fire more often. This is only partially addressed by restricting to features that are “active” somewhere, not when they are active on the evaluated token.
The convergence score relies on top-1 many-to-one matching across seeds; while ablations compare to greedy/Hungarian, the choice may still inflate similarity for broad directions and understate collision issues.
Subspace-angle methodology is under-specified (e.g., subspace dimensionality, orthonormalization, whitening), making it hard to interpret principal-angle magnitudes in high dimensions.


Experimental gaps or methodological issues
Only TopK SAEs are tested, despite known architecture-dependent stability; no ReLU/ℓ1 variants are evaluated.
A single middle layer per model is analyzed; layerwise variability in stability and universality is not explored.
Universality is measured only via max absolute activation correlation; alternatives such as CCA/Procrustes, cross-reconstruction across independently trained SAEs, or USAE-style joint objectives could triangulate the claim more robustly.
The study uses different training corpora for the models (OpenWebText vs. The Pile), which can confound universality signals and may relate to the GPT-2 anomaly reported.


Clarity or presentation issues
Frequent notational inconsistencies (ρ vs. p; “p ≈ 0.345–0.380” apparently meaning ρ; some equations with broken set notation) impede precision.
Table 1 reports an MSE of ≈0.977 alongside explained variance ≈0.99 for GPT-2 Small; units/normalization seem inconsistent and need clarification.
The manuscript alternates between describing the method as “computation-free” or “training-free” while it in fact requires training multiple SAEs per layer per seed.


Missing related work or comparisons
While USAE (Thasarathan et al., 2025) and universality work (Lan et al., 2024) are cited, direct methodological triangulation with their cross-model training or cross-reconstruction metrics is not performed.
Temporal/stability analyses like crosscoder-style alignment across training snapshots (feature emergence and stability) could further contextualize the stability–importance relationship.
sections.detailed_comments:
Technical soundness evaluation
The central correlation between convergence score and cross-model universality (ρ ≈ 0.28–0.68) is compelling and consistent across two of three models; the GPT-2 active-only anomaly is honestly discussed and attributed to data/model differences, but still warrants further probing (e.g., repeat with matched corpora; check layer choice).
The convergence score is a reasonable proxy for seed stability, but its reliance on top-1 matching and decoder cosine alone could be complemented with activation-based matching or joint (decoder+activation) similarity during feature selection, especially as the ablation suggests activation similarity is often a stronger predictor of causal importance.
The causal importance analysis would be more convincing if reported both (a) per-token conditional on feature firing and (b) aggregated with controls for activation frequency and magnitude (e.g., matched pairs by frequency or using partial correlations). As written, the negative Cohen’s d among active features is interesting but small, and plausible confounds remain.
The subspace-angle analysis is an appealing direction, but the procedure needs more detail: how are subspaces constructed (raw decoders vs. orthonormal bases), what dimensions are compared, how many principal angles are averaged, and how is the null constructed given highly unequal core/periphery sizes? Without this, it is difficult to fully trust the magnitude and interpretation of “0.21–0.22 rad vs. null 5th percentile 0.46–0.94.”


Experimental evaluation assessment
The multi-seed, multi-model setup with bootstrapped CIs and ablations is a strong point. However, the very small training budget leaves too many dead features, which: (i) inflates “peripheral” counts with near-dead features; (ii) induces degeneracies in AUC analyses (two models); and (iii) may explain part of the universality vs. activation frequency anti-correlation among active features.
The ROC/AUC results for predicting high-importance features show that a simple LR over several signals reaches very high AUC (~0.95) on active-only analyses; this suggests that multiple simple diagnostics can be combined effectively. It would be valuable to report calibration plots or precision-recall curves, given severe class imbalance, and to verify robustness across thresholds.
The qualitative examples are helpful and consistent with the narrative: core features are broad and frequent; peripheral features are rarer and more specific.


Comparison with related work (using the summaries provided)
USAE (Thasarathan et al., 2025) finds that highly universal concepts often carry large “energy.” This paper’s per-feature KL finding trends in the opposite direction when looking at active features individually. Reconciling these results by evaluating group-wise or cumulative importance (e.g., energy across all core features vs. per-feature KL) would be valuable; the two results may be compatible if core features are collectively crucial but individually redundant.
Cross-snapshot crosscoders (feature alignment over pretraining) show persistence and emergence timing that correlate with causal roles on tasks. Bridging that line of work with the present seed-stability analysis (e.g., are seed-stable features also temporally persistent?) would strengthen the broader interpretability narrative.
Recent steering/feature-selection works (e.g., LangFIR; SteerRM; CB‑SAE) emphasize selecting sparse, high-precision features for control. The finding that rarity correlates with higher per-feature impact fits those observations; connecting the present convergence-based selection to those pipelines could expand applicability.


Discussion of broader impact and significance
The proposed use case—ranking/identifying cross-model universal features using only multi-seed training on a single model—is practical for cross-model interpretability studies and could reduce manual curation.
The multi-dimensional view of “feature quality” is useful: depending on the goal (universality vs. steering), different metrics (convergence vs. frequency) should be prioritized. This guidance is actionable for researchers and practitioners.
Limitations are candidly acknowledged, particularly training-scale constraints and architecture/layer narrowness; addressing them would substantially increase confidence and impact.
sections.questions_for_authors:
How is causal importance computed with respect to tokens where a feature is inactive? Could you report KL conditioned on the feature being active (or controlling for activation frequency/magnitude), and do the core vs. peripheral comparisons persist under that conditioning?
For the subspace-angle analysis, what is the exact subspace dimensionality used per seed, how are bases constructed/orthonormalized, and how many principal angles are summarized? Could you provide sensitivity to subspace dimensionality or a PCA-whitened variant?
The universality metric uses max absolute Pearson correlation of feature activations across models. How sensitive are the results to alternative alignment metrics (e.g., CCA, Procrustes/Orthogonal Procrustes, cross-reconstruction using a linear map, or USAE-style joint training)?
The GPT-2 active-only universality correlation is near zero. If you evaluate all three models on the same held-out corpus and (ideally) matched tokenization, do the trends converge? Could you add a matched-corpus ablation?
Can you disentangle liveness from stability more explicitly, e.g., by stratifying features into narrow activation-frequency bins and recomputing the convergence–universality correlations within bins?
Do your conclusions hold for ReLU/ℓ1 SAEs or for different sparsity levels (k), and across earlier/later layers? Even a reduced experiment (one model, three seeds) would help generalize the claims.
The paper sometimes describes the method as “computation-free” or “training-free,” but it requires training multiple SAEs. Can you clarify the compute requirements and when convergence scoring is advantageous in practice compared to cross-model matching?
sections.overall_assessment:
Estimated Score:
                    5.6/10
                    (Calibrated to ICLR scale)
                
            This paper tackles a timely and important question in mechanistic interpretability: what makes an SAE feature “good,” and how do seed stability, cross-model universality, and causal importance relate? The core empirical finding—that seed-stable features are markedly more universal across models—is novel and actionable, and the dissociation with per-feature causal importance is insightful, pushing the community toward multi-dimensional evaluation of feature quality. The work is weakened by a constrained training budget leading to very high dead-feature fractions, some methodological under-specification (especially in the subspace-angle analysis and causal-importance conditioning), and notational inconsistencies that occasionally impede clarity. Despite these issues, the main result appears robust on two of three models, the ablations are thoughtful, and the paper is well-motivated with clear implications. I view this as a solid, forward-moving contribution that would benefit from added analyses to control for activity confounds, matched-corpus checks, and a brief ReLU/ℓ1 SAE sanity test. With such revisions, I would be comfortable recommending acceptance; as it stands, I lean toward a weak accept given the novelty and potential utility to the community.
